The planets are our siblings in space. - The solar system is a gravitationally bound planetary system consisting of the Sun, eight planets, five officially recognized dwarf planets, hundreds of moons and millions of smaller bodies - The eight planets in our solar system, listed in order from the Sun outward, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune.

- A neighborhood is a small, familiar, local area within a larger community. - In astronomy, "our cosmic neighborhood" refers to the region of space immediately surrounding Earth—the Solar System and the nearest stars. - At a cosmic level our neighborhood is our Solar System. - At a human level our neighborhood is the town we live in.

- An orbit is the curved path that one object takes around another object due to gravity. - Newton's cannonball example: 1. Imagine a cannon on a very tall mountain. 2. Fire a cannonball. It falls to the ground. 3. Fire it faster. It lands farther away. 4. Fire it fast enough. It falls around the Earth—always falling, always missing. That is an orbit. - An orbit is the gravitationally curved trajectory of a smaller body around a larger body, described mathematically by Kepler's laws of planetary motion and Newton's law of universal gravitation. - An orbit is the art of falling without ever landing.

- The Sun is the star at the center of our solar system. - The Sun is a massive sphere of hot plasma that provides the energy for nearly all life on Earth. - The Suns gravity holds the solar system together. - The Suns light warms the Earth and powers photosynthesis (the basis of the food chain). - The Suns energy drives weather, ocean currents and the climate. - The Suns energy source is nuclear fusion (converting hydrogen into helium). - The Sun is about 1.39 million kilometers (864,000 miles) — 109 times Earth's diameter. - The Sun is the reason we can see, the reason we are warm and the reason anything grows. - Every meal we have ever eaten is stored sunlight. - Earth is about 150 million kilometers (93 million miles) from the Sun. - The Sun is composed primarily of hydrogen (73%) and helium (25%).

- A planet is a large, round celestial body that orbits a star. - A planet is not a star itself and has cleared its orbital neighborhood of other debris. - If a star is a parent, a planet is a child. - The three criteria for being a planet: 1. Orbits a star. 2. Is round. Its own gravity pulls it into a sphere. 3. Cleared its orbit. It is the dominant gravitational body in its neighborhood. - What are not planets: - Dwarf planets (like Pluto): Pluto orbits the Sun and is round but has not cleared its orbits. - Moons: Orbit planets, not stars. - Asteroids: Not round.

- Earth is the third planet from the Sun. - If the universe is a vast ocean, Earth is your boat. - The Earth diameter is about 12,742 kilometers (7,918 miles). - Earth is about 150 million kilometers (93 million miles) from the Sun. - Eath is 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen. - Earth has a magnetic field that protects us from solar wind, an atmosphere that shields us from radiation and a Moon that stabilizes our seasons. - Earth is the ground we stand on, the air we breathe and has and abundant amount of surface water.
- World is Earth.

- Celestial refers to anything outside Earth's atmosphere. - Celestial includes stars, planets, moons, comets, galaxies and the space between them. - Celestial is of the sky (stars, planets, moons, galaxies), terrestrial is of the land (mountains, forests, deserts, grasslands, soil) and aquatic is of the water (oceans, lakes, rivers, streams, swamps).

- Terrestrial means "of or relating to the Earth" or "living on land". - Terrestrial is the ground we walk on. - Terrestrial is everything that is not sky and not water. - Celestial is of the sky (stars, planets, moons, galaxies), terrestrial is of the land (mountains, forests, deserts, grasslands, soil) and aquatic is of the water (oceans, lakes, rivers, streams, swamps).

- Aquatic means of or relating to water. It is the opposite of terrestrial (land) and aerial (air). - Types of aquatic organisms (who lives in water): - Fish: Live in water their whole lives (examples: salmon, trout, clownfish, sharks) - Mammals: Breathe air but live in or near water (examples: whales, dolphins, seals, otters) - Reptiles: Some live in water, some visit (examples: sea turtles, alligators, sea snakes) - Amphibians: Start in water (as tadpoles), live on land as adults (examples: frogs, salamanders, newts) - Plants: Grow in or near water (examples: seaweed, algae, water lilies, cattails) - Invertebrates: No backbone (examples: crabs, lobsters, clams, jellyfish, coral) - Microorganisms: Microscopic, form the base of aquatic food webs (examples: phytoplankton, zooplankton, bacteria) - Aquatic is any water (fresh or salt) and marine is salt water only. - Aquatic is the world of water—the place where fish swim, rivers flow and your bodies becomes buoyant.

- A mountain is a large landform that rises significantly above the surrounding terrain, typically with steep slopes, a defined summit and an elevation of at least 300 meters (1,000 feet) above the surrounding area. - A mountain is the Earth reaching up to touch the sky. It is where the air gets thin, the snow may stay year-round and the world looks small beneath us.

- A forest is a large area of land dominated by trees. - A forest includes a living community of plants, animals, fungi and microorganisms that forms a complex, interconnected ecosystem. - Forest Purpose: - Produce oxygen: Trees release oxygen during photosynthesis. - Absorb carbon dioxide: Trees store carbon in their wood, helping regulate the climate. - Provide habitat: 80% of terrestrial biodiversity lives in forests. - Regulate water: Forests absorb rain, reduce flooding and release water slowly. - Prevent erosion: Tree roots hold soil in place. - Cool the air: Trees provide shade and release water vapor (transpiration). - A forest is a quiet cathedral of green. It is the lungs of the planet, the home of countless creatures and the place we go to remember that we are not alone.

- Fresh water is water that contains very low concentrations of dissolved salts. - Fresh water is typically less than 1 gram of salt per liter (less than 0.1% salt). - Only about 2.5% of Earth's water is fresh water. Of that, most is frozen in ice caps and glaciers. The fresh water available in lakes, rivers and groundwater is a tiny fraction of a tiny fraction. - Fresh water is the water we drink, the water that grows our food and the water that keeps us alive. It is rare, precious and worth protecting.

- Salt water is water that contains significant concentrations of dissolved salts. - Salt water is typically about 35 grams of salt per liter (3.5% salt) in the open ocean. - Why the ocean is salty: - Rain falls on land, slightly acidic. - The rain erodes rocks, dissolving minerals (including salt). - Rivers carry these dissolved minerals to the ocean. - Water evaporates from the ocean, leaving the salts behind. - Over billions of years, the salts have accumulated. - Drinking seawater dehydrates us and can lead to kidney failure and/or death. - Salt water is water with dissolved salt concentration typically 30-50 g/L (3-5%), primarily sodium chloride, comprising 96.5% of Earth's water, found in oceans and seas and unsuitable for human consumption without desalination.

- The atmosphere is the layer of gases that surrounds a planet. - The atmosphere is held in place by gravity. - Earth's atmosphere is the thin, life-giving blanket of air that wraps around our planet. - If Earth is an apple, the atmosphere is the skin—thin, fragile and absolutely essential. - The atmosphere is the only thing standing between us and the vacuum of space. - The layers of Earth's atmosphere (from Earth's surface up): - Troposphere: Where we live, includes weather, clouds and us. - Stratosphere: Includes the ozone layer and flying airplanes. - Mesosphere: Meteors burn up here. - Thermosphere: Includes Auroras (Northern Lights) and the International Space Station. - Exosphere: The very edge, fading into space. - What the atmosphere does: - Gives us air to breathe. - Protects you from the Sun by blocking harmful UV radiation. - Burns up meteors b/c friction in the mesosphere destroys space rocks before they hit the ground. - Keeps us warm by traping heat near the surface (the greenhouse effect). - Carries water by moving water vapor around the planet (the water cycle). - An atmosphere is a layer of gases retained by a planet's gravity, consisting on Earth primarily of nitrogen (78%), oxygen (21%), argon (0.9%) and trace gases including carbon dioxide and water vapor.

- Science Perspective: - A vacuum is a space completely empty of matter. - A vacuum has no atoms, no molecules, no particles. - The vacuum of space is the near-emptiness between stars and planets. - The vacuum of space is the silence between stars. It is the reason you cannot hear the Sun (it is screaming but there is no air to carry the sound). - Cleaning Perspective: - A vacuum cleaner is a device that sucks up dirt. - A vacuum cleaner works by creating a partial vacuum (lower pressure inside the machine), so normal air pressure outside pushes air and dust into the machine.

- The greenhouse effect is the natural process by which certain gases in Earth's atmosphere trap heat. - The greenhouse effect keeps the planet warm enough for life to exist. - Greenhouse Effect Steps: 1. The Sun sends energy to Earth. Most is visible light (short wavelength). 2. Earth absorbs some of this energy and warms up. 3. Earth radiates heat back toward space. This heat is infrared radiation (long wavelength). 4. Greenhouse gases (CO₂, methane, water vapor) absorb some of this infrared heat and trap it in the atmosphere. 5. The trapped heat warms the planet. This is the natural greenhouse effect. - The greenhouse effect is the reason why we are not living on a frozen rock but humans are adding too many greenhouse gases, making the blanket very thick.

- Vapor is a gas that is normally a liquid or solid at room temperature and pressure. - Vapor is the gaseous state of a substance that we usually think of as a liquid. - Gas is any substance in the gaseous state and vapor is the gaseous state of a substance that is usually liquid or solid - Common vapors: - Water vapor: Clouds, steam, humidity. - Gasoline vapor: What we smell at the gasoline pump (flammable). - Mercury vapor: Toxic gas from the metal (used in old thermometers). - Iodine vapor: Purple gas when solid iodine is heated. - Vapor is the gaseous phase of a substance that exists as a liquid or solid under standard conditions, characterized by the ability to condense into its condensed phase through pressure increase or temperature decrease without crossing a phase boundary.

- Air is the mixture of gases that makes up Earth's atmosphere. - Air is the invisible stuff we breathe, move through and feel as wind. - What air is made of (by volume): - Nitrogen: - 78% of air is nitrogen. - Essential for soil and life. - Oxygen: - 21% of air is nitrogen. - What we breathe. - Argon: - 0.9% of air is argon. - Does nothing (just there). - Carbon dioxide: - 0.04% of air is carbon dioxide. - Essential for soil and life. - Air has mass b/c a room full of air weighs about 50 kilograms (110 pounds).

- Breath is the air that moves into and out of our lungs during breathing. - Breath is the physical substance of life. - Breath is the invisible tide that connects our inner world to the outer world. - Breathe is the verb (the action), breath is the noun (the thing being moved). - Breath is the air itself, on its journey through us. - The Journey of One Breath: 1. A molecule of oxygen drifts in the air, exhaled moments ago by a tree. 2. An inhale happens through the nose or mouth. The air is warmed, filtered and moistened. 3. Air rushes down the throat. 4. Air splits into smaller and smaller passages in the lungs, bronchi and bronchioles. 5. Oxygen crosses a thin membrane into the blood. 6. Red blood cells carry the oxygen to every cell in the body. 7. Within red blood cells the oxygen is used to release energy from food which creates carbon dioxide. 8. The red blood cells carry carbon dioxide back to your lungs. 9. The lungs release the carbon dioxide w/ an exhale. 10. A plant inhales the carbon dioxide and uses it to grow. - We can change our breath to change our emotion. Emotional state: - Calm: Slow, deep, steady breath - Anxious: Fast, shallow, irregular breath - Excited: Quick, light breath - Angry: Short, forceful, hot breath - Relieved: A deep inhale, then a long exhale (a sigh of relief) - Breath is the noun form of breathing—the air itself on its journey through a living body. It contains oxygen (inhaled) and carbon dioxide (exhaled).

- Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is a colorless, odorless gas made of one carbon atom covalently bonded to two oxygen atoms. - Carbon dioxide is a natural part of Earth's atmosphere and a key player in photosynthesis, respiration and climate. - The Journey of One Breath: 1. A molecule of oxygen drifts in the air, exhaled moments ago by a tree. 2. An inhale happens through the nose or mouth. The air is warmed, filtered and moistened. 3. Air rushes down the throat. 4. Air splits into smaller and smaller passages in the lungs, bronchi and bronchioles. 5. Oxygen crosses a thin membrane into the blood. 6. Red blood cells carry the oxygen to every cell in the body. 7. Within red blood cells the oxygen is used to release energy from food which creates carbon dioxide. 8. The red blood cells carry carbon dioxide back to your lungs. 9. The lungs release the carbon dioxide w/ an exhale. 10. A plant inhales the carbon dioxide and uses it to grow. - Carbon dioxide is the breath we give to plants.

- Nitrogen (N₂) is a colorless, odorless, tasteless gas that makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere. - Nitrogen is the most abundant gas in the air we breathe. - Nitrogen is the invisible majority. We breathe it in and out with every breath. It does nothing to us and that is its job. - Without nitrogen, the air would be 100% oxygen—everything would burn much faster and fires would be unstoppable. - Nitrogen's mass helps maintain atmospheric pressure. - Plants need nitrogen to grow (but they cannot use N₂ from the air—bacteria in soil alter nitrogen into usable forms).

- Oxygen (O₂) is a colorless, odorless, tasteless gas that makes up about 21% of Earth's atmosphere. - Oxygen is the gas that almost all animals need to breathe in order to live. - The Journey of One Breath: 1. A molecule of oxygen drifts in the air, exhaled moments ago by a tree. 2. An inhale happens through the nose or mouth. The air is warmed, filtered and moistened. 3. Air rushes down the throat. 4. Air splits into smaller and smaller passages in the lungs, bronchi and bronchioles. 5. Oxygen crosses a thin membrane into the blood. 6. Red blood cells carry the oxygen to every cell in the body. 7. Within red blood cells the oxygen is used to release energy from food which creates carbon dioxide. 8. The red blood cells carry carbon dioxide back to your lungs. 9. The lungs release the carbon dioxide w/ an exhale. 10. A plant inhales the carbon dioxide and uses it to grow. - Oxygen is the gas that keeps us alive.

- Argon (Ar) is a colorless, odorless, tasteless noble gas that makes up about 0.9% of Earth's atmosphere. - Argon is completely inert and almost never reacts with anything. - Argon is the invisible third wheel. It is everywhere but it never gets involved. That is not laziness. That is nobility.

- Wind is the natural movement of air across the surface of a planet. - Wind is caused by differences in air pressure, which are caused by uneven heating of the atmosphere by the Sun. - Planetary wind cause: 1. The Sun heats the Earth unevenly (equator gets more heat, poles get less). 2. Warm air rises (it becomes less dense, so it floats upward like a hot air balloon). 3. Rising warm air creates an area of low pressure (less air pushing down). 4. Cool air is denser and heavier, so it sinks, creating an area of high pressure. 5. Air moves from high pressure to low pressure. That movement is wind. - Local wind causes: - Sea breeze: Land heats faster than water during the day; cool air from sea moves inland. - Land breeze: Land cools faster than water at night; cool air from land moves to sea. - Mountain breeze: Air cools on mountains at night and flows down valleys. - Valley breeze: Air warms in valleys during the day and flows up mountains. - Wind is the bulk movement of air in the atmosphere driven by horizontal pressure gradients - Wind is the breath of the planet—the atmosphere stretching and yawning, moving from where it is crowded to where it is empty.

- A satellite is any object that orbits a larger object. - Types of satellites: - Natural: - Natural satellites are called moons. - Examples: Earth's Moon, Jupiter's 79 moons. - Artificial: - Artificial satellites are machines launched into orbit by humans. - Examples: GPS satellites, the International Space Station (ISS), Hubble Space Telescope. - A satellite is an object that orbits a larger body, either natural (a moon) or artificial (a human-made spacecraft placed into orbit for purposes such as communication, navigation or observation).

- The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite. - The moon is a cold, rocky sphere that orbits our planet every 27.3 days. - The moon pulls the tides, provides light to the Earth at night and stabilizes Earth's tilt (which gives us seasons). - Without the Moon, Earth would wobble on its axis and the climate would be wildly unstable. Life as we know it might not exist. - The moon diameter is 3,475 kilometers (2,159 miles), about 1/4 of Earth's size. - The moon is about 384,400 kilometers (238,855 miles) from the Earth.

- A tide is the regular rise and fall of sea levels caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun on Earth's oceans. - Moon's gravity pulls water toward the Moon. - Sun's gravity also pulls on Earth's oceans but less than the Moon does. - As Earth rotates, each location on Earth passes through both bulges each day—giving two high tides and two low tides every 24 hours and 50 minutes. - Near bulge: The side of Earth facing the Moon (high tide). - Far bulge: The side of Earth opposite the Moon (low tide). - A tide is the periodic rise and fall of sea level caused by the gravitational interactions between Earth, the Moon and the Sun, characterized by two high and two low tides per lunar day (24 hours, 50 minutes)

- Heat is the transfer of thermal energy from a hotter object to a colder object. - How heat moves: - Conduction: - Touching (atoms bump into neighbors). - Example: A metal spoon getting hot in soup. - Convection: - Fluids moving (hot rises, cold sinks). - Example: Boiling water, weather systems. - Radiation: - Electromagnetic waves (no contact needed). - Example: The Sun warming our face. - Heat is the transfer of thermal energy between systems due to a temperature difference, governed by the laws of thermodynamics.
- Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. - Temperature is how fast the atoms or molecules are jiggling.

- Celsius (°C) is a temperature scale where 0°C is the freezing point of water, 37°C is a humans body temperature and 100°C is the boiling point of water at sea level. - Formulas (C=Celsius, F=Fahrenheit, K=Kelvin): - °C = (°F - 32) × 5/9 - °C = K - 273.15 - °F = (°C × 9/5) + 32 - K = °C + 273.15 - Most of the world uses Celsius b/c celcius is simple, logical and based on water—the most important substance for life.

- Fahrenheit (°F) is a temperature scale where 32°F is the freezing point of water, 98.6°F is a humans body temperature and 212°F is the boiling point of water at sea level. - Formulas (C=Celsius, F=Fahrenheit, K=Kelvin): - °C = (°F - 32) × 5/9 - °C = K - 273.15 - °F = (°C × 9/5) + 32 - K = °C + 273.15 - Fahrenheit feels more precise for weather b/c a change of 1°F is about half the size of a change of 1°C. For weather, Fahrenheit gives us a finer scale—"78°F vs 79°F" feels more precise than "25.6°C vs 26.1°C."

- A thermometer is a device that measures temperature. - A thermometer uses a physical property that changes predictably with heat, such as the expansion of a liquid, the resistance of a wire or the color of a material. - Most substances expand when heated and contract when cooled. A thermometer uses this expansion to measure temperature. - A thermometer is a temperature measuring instrument.

- Hot/Warm is subjective perceptions of high temperature. - Temperature is objective (measured with a thermometer) and hot/warm are subjective (how it feels to you). - The spectrum of temperature perception: - Cold: Below 10°C (50°F) feels uncomfortably cold. - Chilly: 10-15°C (50-60°F) feels noticeably cold. - Cool: 15-20°C (60-68°F) feels refreshing. - Warm: 20-30°C (68-86°F) feels cozy. - Hot: Above 30°C (86°F) feels uncomfortably hot. - "Hot" and "cold" are subjectively relative b/c 25°C can feel hot to one person and cold to someone else.

- Cold/Chilly is the subjective perceptions of low temperature. - Temperature is objective (measured with a thermometer) and hot/warm are subjective (how it feels to you). - The spectrum of temperature perception: - Cold: Below 10°C (50°F) feels uncomfortably cold. - Chilly: 10-15°C (50-60°F) feels noticeably cold. - Cool: 15-20°C (60-68°F) feels refreshing. - Warm: 20-30°C (68-86°F) feels cozy. - Hot: Above 30°C (86°F) feels uncomfortably hot. - "Hot" and "cold" are subjectively relative b/c 15°C can feel cool to one person and cold to someone else.

- Water (H₂O) is a molecule made of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. - Water exists in three states: liquid (water), solid (ice) and gas (water vapor). - The three states of water: - Solid (ice): - Is rigid and has a fixed shape. - Molecules locked in a crystal lattice. - Temperature is below 0°C (32°F). - Liquid (water): - Flows and takes the shape of its container. - Molecules slide past each other. - Temperature is 0°C to 100°C (32-212°F). - Gas (water vapor, steam): - Is invisible and fills its container. - Molecules fly freely. - Temperature is above 100°C (212°F). - Water expands when it freezes (most substances contract). - Water is what we drink, ice is what we put in our cup and steam is what rises from our morning coffee. - Drinking water every day is essential for staying healthy b/c water helps regulate body temperature, maintain electrolyte balance, support digestion, transport nutrients, and flush out waste products.

- The water cycle is the continuous movement of water on, above and below the surface of Earth. - The water cycle is powered by the Sun and gravity. - The steps of the water cycle: 1. Evaporation: The Sun heats liquid water, turning it into water vapor (gas). 2. Transpiration: Plants release water vapor from their leaves. 3. Condensation: Water vapor cools in the atmosphere, forming clouds. 4. Precipitation: Water falls from clouds as rain, snow, sleet or hail 5. Runoff: Water flows over land (rivers, streams) back to oceans. 6. Infiltration: Water soaks into the ground, becoming groundwater. - The water cycle is the Earth's recycling system. Every drop of water you have ever drunk has been here for billions of years, cycling through the sky, the ground and living things.

- A cycle is a sequence of events that repeats regularly. - A cycle is a circle of cause and effect where the end leads back to the beginning. - A cycle is a closed sequence of events or processes in which the final state influences or determines the initial state, allowing the sequence to repeat indefinitely under consistent conditions. - A cycle is nature's way of making sure nothing is wasted. Water falls, evaporates, falls again. The moon wanes, waxes, wanes again. You wake, sleep, wake again. Circles within circles.

- A system is a set of interconnected parts that work together as a whole. - The key properties of a system: - Interconnected parts: Each part affects the others. - Boundary: Something separates the system from its environment. - Inputs and outputs: The system takes things in and sends things out. - Feedback: The outputs influence the inputs (cycles within the system). - Emergence: The whole system has properties that the parts alone do not. - A system is a bounded set of interconnected components that function as an integrated whole, characterized by inputs, outputs, feedback loops and emergent properties not present in the individual components.

- Recycling is the process of collecting, processing and converting waste materials into new products. - Recyclins is closing the loop of consumption instead of throwing things away. - The recycling loop: 1. Collection: Waste is gathered from homes, businesses and recycling centers. 2. Processing: Materials are sorted, cleaned, shredded and melted. 3. Manufacturing: Processed materials become new products. 4.Purchase: Consumers buy products made from recycled materials (closing the loop). - Why recycling matters: - Less mining, logging and drilling for virgin materials. - Making products from recycled materials uses far less energy. - Less trash buried in the ground. - Less manufacturing pollution, less plastic in the ocean. - Less energy use means less CO₂ emissions. - Recycling is how we mimic nature. In nature, there is no "away." Everything becomes food for something else. Recycling is our attempt to remember that.

- An ocean is a vast, continuous body of salt water that covers most of Earth's surface. - Oceans are the largest and deepest of the planet's aquatic ecosystems. - What the oceans do: - Produces oxygen: Marine phytoplankton (microscopic algae) produce 50-80% of Earth's oxygen. - Absorb carbon dioxide: The ocean absorbs about 30% of human-emitted CO₂ (reducing climate change but causing ocean acidification) - Regulate climate: The ocean stores and transports heat around the planet (the Gulf Stream warms Europe). - Provides food: Fish, shellfish and seaweed feed billions of people. - Supports life: The ocean contains 99% of Earth's living space (by volume). - An ocean is a continuous body of salt water.

- A lake is a body of fresh or salt water surrounded by land. - A lake is not part of the ocean and usually larger and deeper than a pond. - A lake is an inland body of standing water (fresh or saline) occupying a basin, larger and deeper than a pond, with no direct ocean connection, formed by glacial, tectonic, volcanic, fluvial or anthropogenic processes. - A lake is the land's cup of water—still, quiet and patient. It reflects the mountains, holds the fish and gives us a place to skip stones.

A pond is a small, shallow, standing body of fresh water, smaller than a lake, typically with sunlight reaching the bottom throughout, supporting rooted aquatic plants and often seasonal or temporary.

- A river is a large, natural flowing watercourse that moves fresh water from higher ground toward an ocean, a lake or another river. - A river is a natural, gravity-fed watercourse of fresh water flowing from higher to lower elevation within a defined channel. - A river originates at a source (spring, glacier, lake) and terminates at a mouth (ocean, lake or another river), with a drainage basin collecting water from surrounding land.

- A stream is a small, natural flowing watercourse. - A stream is a body of flowing water smaller than a river, often feeding into a river, lake or ocean. - A stream is a small natural watercourse of flowing fresh water, typically smaller than a river originating from springs, seeps or runoff and often serving as a tributary to a larger stream or river.

- Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae and some bacteria use sunlight to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose (sugar) and oxygen. - The Sun is the energy source and photosynthesis is the machine that captures that energy and turns it into food for nearly all life on Earth. - Photosynthesis produces the oxygen we are breathing right now. - Photosynthesis is the foundation of almost every food chain. - Photosynthesis removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere (regulating the climate). - Photosynthesis is how a seed becomes a tree, how sunlight becomes an apple and how we get to breathe.

- Food is any substance consumed by an organism to provide energy, build and repair tissue and sustain life. - The food chain: 1. Producers (examples: plants, algae) make their own food via photosynthesis. 2. Herbivores (examples: cows, rabbits, caterpillars, humans) eat plants. 3. Carnivores (examples: wolves, snakes, hawks) eat herbivores. 4. Top Predators (examples: lions orcas, humans) eat carnivores. 4. Decomposers (examples: fungi, bacteria) break down dead things and return nutrients to the soil. - All food is stored sunlight. 1. The Sun shines. 2. A plant captures that sunlight through photosynthesis and stores it as sugar. 3. We eat the plant (or eat an animal that ate the plant). 4. Our body releases that stored sunlight as energy. - Food is any organic substance consumed to provide nutritional support for an organism, containing macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) and micronutrients (vitamins, minerals) that supply energy and enable metabolic function. - Lovely food options include: - Pumfu - Hummus - Berries - Avocados - Oats & Oatmeal - Beans & Lentils - Spinach & Spring Mix - Brown Rice, Quinoa & Sweet Potatoes - Peanut Butter & Other Nut Butters

Life is a characteristic that distinguishes physical entities that maintain homeostasis, are organized, grow, respond to stimuli, reproduce and evolve—from those that do not. - The seven characteristics of life (what makes something alive): 1. Organization: - Living things are made of cells (the basic unit of life). - Examples: A leaf, a bacterium, humans. 2. Homeostasis: - Maintaining a stable internal environment. - Example: The human body stays at 37°C (98.6°F). 3. Metabolism: - Using energy (eating, breathing, growing). - Examples: A tree captures sunlight; humans eat food. 4. Growth: - Getting bigger or more complex over time. - Example: A puppy becomes a dog. 5. Response to stimuli: - Reacting to the environment. - Examples: A flower turns toward the sun; we pull our hand from a hot stove. 6. Reproduction: - Making more of our kind. - Examples: A cat has kittens; a bacterium splits in two. 7. Evolution: - Changing over generations. - Example: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria. - Life is the thing that makes a seed become a tree, a caterpillar become a butterfly and a baby become a grandmother.

- An organism is any individual living thing—from a single bacterium to a giant sequoia tree to a human being. - The scale of organisms (from smallest to largest): 1. Unicellular: - Single-celled. - Size: Microscopic (0.5-100 micrometers). - Examples: Bacteria, amoeba, yeast. 2. Small multicellular: - Size: Tiny (millimeters to centimeters). - Examples: Ant, worm, moss. 3 . Medium multicellular: - Size: Centimeters to meters. - Examples: Mouse, rose bush, cat. 4. Large multicellular: - Size: Meters to tens of meters. - Examples: Human, oak tree, whale. 5. Very large: - Size: Hundreds of meters (in groups). - Examples: Aspen grove (connected root system, one organism). - What all organisms share: - They are made of cells (at least one). - They maintain homeostasis (internal balance). - They metabolize (use energy). - They respond to their environment. - They reproduce (or are part of a species that does). - An organism is a contiguous living system capable of carrying out the fundamental processes of life, composed of one or more cells and bounded by a physical structure that separates it from and unites it to its environment.

- Carbon is a chemical element that is the fundamental building block of organic chemistry and life as we know it. - Why carbon is special: - Four bonds: Carbon can attach to up to four other atoms, creating complex, branching and ring-shaped molecules. - Bonds to itself: Carbon can form long chains, rings and sheets (diamond, graphite, graphene). - Stable but flexible: Carbon molecules can be strong (diamond) or soft (graphite), rigid (cellulose) or flexible (plastic). - What carbon makes: - Carbohydrates: - Examples: Sugars, starches, cellulose. - Found in: Plants, food, energy. - Proteins: - Examples: Enzymes, muscles, hair. - Found in: All living things. - Lipids (fats): - Examples: Oils, cell membranes, hormones. - Found in: All living things. - Nucleic acids: - Examples: DNA, RNA. - Found in: Genetic code (you). - Hydrocarbons: - Examples: Oil, natural gas, plastic. - Found in: Fossil fuels. - About 18% of the human body is carbon (by mass). - We are a carbon-based life form.

- Nutrients are substances that organisms need to survive, grow and reproduce. - Nutrients is the chemical building blocks and energy sources of life. - The two types of nutrients: - Macronutrients: - Provide: Energy and bulk materials. - Examples: Carbohydrates, proteins, fats, water. - Micronutrients: - Provide: Vitamins and minerals (small amounts but essential). - Examples: Vitamin C, iron, calcium, zinc. - Nutrients are chemical compounds required for the metabolic processes of an organism, classified as macronutrients (required in large amounts) and micronutrients (required in trace amounts), essential for growth, maintenance and reproduction.

Soil is the loose, upper layer of Earth's surface where plants grow. - Soil is a living mixture of broken rock organic matter (decayed plants and animals), water, air and countless tiny organisms. - What soil is made of (the five ingredients): - Mineral particles (sand, silt, clay): - 45% of soil. - Provides structure, holds nutrients. - Water: - 25% of soil. - Dissolves nutrients so roots can absorb them. - Air: - 25% of soil. - Provides oxygen for roots and soil organisms - Organic matter (humus): - 5% of soil. - Decayed plants and animals—the dark, rich part that feeds life. - Living organisms. - Less then 1% of soil. - Bacteria, fungi, worms, insects—the soil is alive - One teaspoon of healthy soil contains more microorganisms than there are people on Earth. - Earthworms, fungi, bacteria and insects are constantly breaking down organic matter and cycling nutrients.

- A cell is the smallest structural and functional unit of life. - A cell is a tiny, self-contained bag of chemistry that can maintain homeostasis, metabolize, grow and reproduce. - What all cells have in common: - Cell membrane: The outer boundary—controls what enters and leaves. - Cytoplasm: The jelly-like fluid inside. - DNA: The genetic instructions (blueprint). - Ribosomes: Machines that build proteins. - Metabolism: Chemical reactions that release and use energy. - The human body has about 30 trillion human cells and about 30 trillion bacterial cells. - A cell is a membrane-bound compartment containing genetic material (DNA or RNA), ribosomes and metabolic machinery, capable of independent replication and energy transformation.

- Bacteria are single-celled prokaryotic organisms. - Bacteria is tiny, ancient and everywhere. - Bacteria is the most abundant form of life on Earth. - Bacteria reproduce by binary fission (splitting in two). - Bacteria live everywhere. - Bacteria are 3.5 billion years old (the oldest life on Earth). - Type of Bacteria: - Beneficial (good): - Help digest food, produce vitamins, decompose waste, fix nitrogen for plants. - Examples: Lactobacillus (yogurt), E. coli (gut health), Rhizobium (soil). - Harmful (pathogenic): - Cause disease. - Examples: Streptococcus (strep throat), Salmonella (food poisoning). - Neutral: - Just exist. - Example: Most bacteria. - The human body has about 30 trillion human cells and about 30 trillion bacterial cells.

- A caterpillar is the larval stage of butterflies and moths. - A caterpillar is a specialized, eating-focused life stage designed to grow rapidly before transforming into an adult. - A caterpillar is a stomach with legs. It exists to eat, to grow and to trust that something beautiful is coming.

- A butterfly is the adult stage of certain insects in the order Lepidoptera. - Butterflies are characterized by large, often brightly colored wings, a proboscis for drinking nectar and a life focused on reproduction and pollination. - Butterfly life cycle (metamorphosis): 1. Egg: - A tiny egg laid on a host plant. - Duration: days to weeks. 2. Caterpillar (larva): - Eats constantly, grows rapidly. - Duration: weeks to months. 3. Pupa (chrysalis): - Inside a protective shell, the caterpillar's body dissolves and reorganizes into a butterfly. - Duration: days to months. 4. Butterfly (adult): - Emerges, dries its wings, flies, drinks nectar, mates, lays eggs. - Duration: days to weeks. - The miracle inside the chrysalis: - The caterpillar does not just grow wings. - Its body breaks down into a soupy mass of cells and those cells reorganize into an entirely different creature. - The same DNA, a different body. - A butterfly is a caterpillar's second act. A butterfly is proof that change is possible, growth is possible and metamorphosis is possible.

- A family is the foundational social unit. - A family is typically consisting of two parents (a father and a mother) and their children, living together and supporting one another emotionally, socially and economically. - A family is the primary source of nurture, moral guidance and cultural tradition within communities.

- A baby is a very young child, especially one who is newly or recently born. - A baby is the natural and precious result of the loving, intimate union between a husband and a wife, representing the continuation of the family line and a shared legacy.

- A parent is a father or a mother. - A father provides strength, protection and guidance. - A mother provides nurturing, comfort and emotional warmth. - Together parents form a complementary pair whose different but equal roles are essential for raising a child to become a healthy, well-adjusted adults.

- A child is the son or daughter of a parent. - A child is born from the union of a father and a mother. - A child represents the future of the family, receiving love, discipline and instruction from both parents in order to learn how to eventually form their own family.

- Marriage is a sacred and legal covenant between a man and a woman. - Marriage is publicly committing to a lifelong, exclusive union. - Deciding to marry someone is a personal choice that should feel like a natural next step rather than a forced decision, indicating that both partners share similar values, life goals and a deep emotional connection. - The purpose of marriage is mutual companionship, emotional support and the procreation and raising of children within a stable, loving home.

- Love is a selfless, enduring commitment to choose the highest good of another person. - Love begins as a deep emotional attraction and develops into a steady, unselfish devotion. - Love is less about a fleeting feeling and more about a steadfast choice to remain faithful, supportive and protective.

- Romance is the set of gestures, words and attentions that express love and affection between a man and a woman. - Romance is the "courtly" phase of a relationship. - Rmance includes the chivalrous acts, thoughtful gifts and intentional quality time that keep the emotional bond alive. - Romance leads toward the commitment of marriage.

- A relationship is the ongoing, dynamic interaction between two or more people, characterized by a pattern of communication, behavior and mutual expectation. - A healthy traditional relationship is built on respect, trust, open communication and a shared vision—whether that vision is building a family (romantic), raising a child (parent-child), supporting one another through life (siblings) or working toward a common goal (friends, colleagues). - A relationship is any bond between people that matters. Your mother, your best friend, your neighbor, your coworker—all are relationships. Each has its own shape, its own duties and its own gifts.

- Connection is the deep, unspoken understanding and emotional bond between two people. - Connection is the sense of being on the same team. - Connection is often felt in a glance across a crowded room or a shared moment of silence. - Connection is the invisible thread that keeps a couple united through hardship, created over time through shared experiences, vulnerability and consistent faithfulness.

- Male refers to the sex of an organism that produces small, mobile gametes (sperm). - In humans, male is one of the two biological sexes, characterized by XY chromosomes, testes and higher levels of testosterone.

- A man is an adult human male. - A man is called to strength, protection, provision and leadership. - Men use their physical and emotional strength to serve and protect their family and community. - A man is not defined by age alone (18 or 21) but by character, responsibility and the willingness to bear burdens for others.

- A boy is a young human male. - A male is a boye from birth through adolescence. - A boy is in training to become a man by learning responsibility, discipline, respect and the skills he will need as a husband and father. - A boy is a man in the making. He needs guidance, example and patience. - Common names for boys in America: Michael, Jonathan, Christopher, James, Noah and William

- Masculine refers to the qualities, traits and roles traditionally associated with men and boys. - Masculine traits include strength (physical and moral), courage, protectiveness, provision, decisiveness and emotional steadiness. - Masculine is not the opposite of feminine. It is a different shape of virtue—complementary, not competitive.
- "He" is a third-person singular pronoun used to refer to a male person or animal. - Examples: He is my brother. He works hard. He loves his family.
- "Him" is the objective case of "he". - Him is used when the male person is the object of a verb or preposition. - Examples: I gave the book to him. She sat beside him. She loves him.

- A husband is a married man. - A husband is joined to his wife in a lifelong covenant of love, fidelity and mutual support. - The husband is called to be the head of the household—not as a tyrant but as a servant-leader who lays down his own comfort for the good of his wife and his children. - A husband is a protector, a provider, a partner and a friend. - A husband leads by serving.

- A boyfriend is a man or boy in a romantic courtship relationship with a woman or girl. - A boyfriend-girlfriend relationship is a season of learning about each other's character, values and suitability for marriage. The relationship is a trial, not a final destination. - A boyfriend is a potential husband.

- A father is a male parent. - A father is called to provide for, protect and guide his children—teaching them right from wrong, modeling manhood and giving his daughter a standard by which she will measure future suitors and his son a model to become. - A father is not just a sperm donor. He is a presence, a voice, a hand on the shoulder and a steady example.

- A brother is a male sibling. - A brother is sharing one or both parents with another child. - A brother is a protector, a rival, a friend and a lifelong companion. - Older brothers are called to watch over younger siblings. - Younger brothers are called to respect and learn from older ones. - A brother is the first friend and the oldest rival. The bond of blood is irreplaceable.

- A grandfather is the father of one's parent. - A grandfather is a source of wisdom, family history and gentle authority. - A grandfather has finished the hard work of raising children and now enjoys the fruit of his labor in the loving of his children and grandchildren—teaching them, supporting them and passing down the family name, values and stories. - A grandfather is the root of the family tree. He has seen more, lived more and loves with a quieter, more patient love.

- Female refers to the sex of an organism that produces large, immobile gametes (eggs). - In humans, female is one of the two biological sexes, characterized by XX chromosomes, ovaries and higher levels of estrogen and progesterone.

- A woman is an adult human female. - A woman is called for nurture, wisdom and hospitality. - Woman use their emotional intelligence and resilience to create a home, raise children and support their husband. - A woman is not defined by age alone but by character, grace and the quiet strength that holds families together.

- A girl is a young human female. - A female is a girl from birth through adolescence. - A girl is in training to become a woman—learning kindness, diligence, modesty and the skills she will need as a wife and mother. - A girl is a woman in the making. She needs protection, guidance and the example of a loving mother and father. - Common names for girls in America: Sophia, Olivia, Charlotte, Rose, Ava and Emma

- Feminine refers to the qualities, traits and roles traditionally associated with women and girls. - Feminine traits include nurturing, gentleness, empathy, hospitality, beauty and emotional depth. - Feminine is not the opposite of masculine. Feminine is a different shape of virtue—complementary, not competitive.
- "She" is a third-person singular pronoun used to refer to a female person or animal. Examples: She is my sister. She sings beautifully. She loves her family.

- Her is the objective case of "she". - Her is used when the female person is the object of a verb or preposition. - Her is also the possessive form. - Objective Examples: I gave the gift to her. He sat beside her. - Possessive Examples: That is her book. Her smile is warm.

- A wife is a married woman. - A wife is joined to her husband in a lifelong covenant of love, fidelity and mutual support. - A wife is called to be the heart of the home—creating warmth, beauty order and supporting her husband as his helpmate and partner. - A wife is a helper, a counselor, a friend and a lover. She is not less than her husband but different—and together they are complete.

- A girlfriend is a woman or girl in a romantic courtship relationship with a man or boy. - The boyfriend-girlfriend relationship is a season of learning about each other's character, values and suitability for marriage. The relationship is a trial, not a final destination. - A girlfriend is a potential wife.

- A mother is a female parent. - A mother is called to nurture, teach and shape her children—giving them emotional security, a moral foundation and the first experience of unconditional love. - A mother is the first face a child sees, the first voice a child hears and the first love a child knows. - A mothers nfluence lasts a lifetime.

- A sister is a female sibling. - A female shares one or both parents with another child. - A sister is a companion, a confidante, a helper and sometimes a rival. - Older sisters are called to watch over younger siblings. - Younger sisters are called to respect and learn from older ones. - A sister is the first friend and the first mirror. - A sister knows your history because she lived it with you.

- A grandmother is the mother of one's parent. - A grandmother is a source of warmth, stories and unconditional acceptance. - A grandmother She has finished the hard work of raising children and now delights in her grandchildren—baking cookies, telling old tales and loving without having to discipline. - A grandmother is the heart of the family's memory.

- Rhythm is the pattern of durations and intervals in time. - Rhythm is the arrangement of sounds, movements or events into a repeating or meaningful sequence. - Key properties of rhythm: - Beat: The basic unit of time, the pulse you tap your foot to. - Tempo: The speed of the beat (fast or slow). - Accent: Emphasis on certain beats (strong/weak, loud/soft). - Meter: The grouping of beats into repeating patterns (e.g., 3/4 waltz, 4/4 march). - Duration: How long a note or event lasts. - Silence (rest): The absence of sound, which gives rhythm its shape. - Rhythm is the skeleton of time. Without it, music is noise, speech is babble and life is chaos. - Our heart has rhythm, our breath has rhythm, our days have rhythm and we are rhythm.
Mind is our conscious and unconsious awareness.

- Mindset is the cognitive filter that is created by your heart's rhythmic pattern. - A chaotic, stressed or negative emotional state produces an erratic heart rhythm (incoherence), which biases perception (creates a mindset) toward threat, limitation and reactivity. - A calm, appreciative or compassionate state produces a smooth ordered heart rhythm (coherence), which biases perception (creates a mindset) toward openness, creativity and connection. - Mindset is the heart-rhythm dependent, prevailing cognitive attitude.

- Conscious is the state of awareness associated with the brain's logical, sequential and voluntary processing. - Conscious is characterized by predictability, intentional control and the ability to generate specific outputs from specific inputs. - Conscious vs Unconscious: - Conscious: - Logical Reasoning / Brain / Linear / Local / Yang / Order / Predictable - Example: I desire to think of a red sports car and then I start imagining a red sports car. - Unconscious: - Intuitive Receiving / Heart / Non-Linear / Non-Local / Yin / Chaos / Unpredictable - Examples: - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and I receive no intuitive guidance. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a feelings that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a dream about a river flowing into the ocean which is symbolic, indirect and open to interpretation. - I am taking a shower, so I desire to get clean but I receive a random feeling that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry.

- The unconscious is the non-conscious aspect of the mind associated with the heart's intuitive, non-linear, spontaneous processing. - The unconscious is characterized the ability to produce outputs without specific inputs or in response to inputs in unexpected ways. - Conscious vs Unconscious: - Conscious: - Logical Reasoning / Brain / Linear / Local / Yang / Order / Predictable - Example: I desire to think of a red sports car and then I start imagining a red sports car. - Unconscious: - Intuitive Receiving / Heart / Non-Linear / Non-Local / Yin / Chaos / Unpredictable - Examples: - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and I receive no intuitive guidance. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a feelings that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a dream about a river flowing into the ocean which is symbolic, indirect and open to interpretation. - I am taking a shower, so I desire to get clean but I receive a random feeling that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry.
The subconscious is the unconscious.
An archetype is a universal, inherited and primordial pattern of thought, image, emotion and behavior.
Psyche is mind.

- Consciousness is the fundamental subjective experience. - Consciousness is "what it is like" to be someone. - Consciousness is the fact that there is something it feels like to be you right now. - While consciousness is the capacity for experience, awareness is the current contents of that experience.

- Awareness is the scope of what is present within ones consciousness. - If consciousness is the stage, awareness is the total amount of light illuminating the stage. - Awareness can be broad (taking in the whole room) or narrow (absorbed in a single thought). - While consciousness is the capacity for experience, awareness is the current contents of that experience.

- Focus is the selective concentration of awareness on a specific object, while excluding others. - Focus is the mechanism that allocates cognitive resources to what is most important to us right now. - Focus is the spotlight of our attention that brings details within our awareness into high resolution clarity.

- Clarity is the quality of being easy to understand. - Example: - Unclear: The event will happen at some point in the near future. - Clear: The event will happen on Tuesday at 3 PM. - Unclear: She felt a certain way about the thing. - Clear: She was ecstatic about the her crush asking her on a date. - What creates clarity? - Simple words. - Short sentences. - Specific examples. - Logical order (first this, then that). - Clarity is free from confusion, ambiguity or obscurity.

- Heart: - The heart is an organ found in humans and other animals that pumps blood through blood vessels. - The heart is far more than a mechanical pump. - The heart is the body's most powerful biological oscillator, generating the largest rhythmic electromagnetic field—up to 100 times stronger electrically and 5,000 times stronger magnetically than the brain. This field entrains (synchronizes) other bodily systems, including the brain. - The heart is also a sensory organ and a information-processing center. - The heart has its own intrinsic nervous system that learns, remembers and makes functional decisions independent of the cranial brain. - The heart is the body's primary rhythmic oscillator and a sophisticated information processing center. - The heart detects intuitive (non-local/non-linear) information before the brain does, then transmits it to the brain. Heart coherence (smooth heart rate variability, generate a sincere feeling of appreciation, care or compassion) enhances intuitive accuracy and cognitive function. - Heart Brain: - The heart brain refers to the heart's intrinsic nervous system that is complex network of approximately 40,000 neurons (nerve cells) located within the heart wall. - The heart brain allows the heart to learn, remember, sense and make functional decisions independently of the cranial brain. - The heart brain communicates with the cranial brain via neural pathways (mostly afferent, meaning heart → brain), hormonal signals and electromagnetic fields. - The heart brain is the neural substrate for heart-based intuition and the generation of coherent rhythmic patterns. - The heart brain is the heart's own complex neural network, enabling it to process information and influence the cranial brain. - Energetic Heart: - The energetic heart refers to the heart's dynamic, radiating electromagnetic field. - The heart's electromagnetic field acts as a central synchronizing signal that influences the brain (entrainment). - The energetic heart creates a toroidal (donut-shaped) energy field that extends outside the body, measurable several feet away with sensitive magnetometers. - The hearts field carries bioelectromagnetic information about a person's emotional and physiological state. - Through our hearts field, the energetic heart can interact with and influence the fields of other people at a distance, especially when both are in a state of coherence. - The energetic heart is the heart's biomagnetic field that carries an energy signature that radiates several feet from our body. - Heartfelt: - Refers to experiences that are deeply authentic, emotionally sincere and arising from a place of genuine feeling rather than intellectual calculation. - Examples: - A heartfelt apology: They speaker feels remorse and their words align w/ this feeling. - A heartfelt decision: The heart's intuitive signal says "yes" (e.g., I know it in my heart). - A heartfelt feeling: He feels remorse (e.g., I have a guilty conscience, I know what I did was wrong, I don't feel good). - A heartfelt feeling: She feels lovely (e.g., I feel so light, I feel so seen, I feel so loved). - Not Heartfelt Example: "I'm sorry." (said automatically w/ no remorse felt). - Heartfelt is often accompanied by a somatic sensation (felt in body) in the chest area (felt in heart). - Brain vs Heart: - Brain: - Logical Reasoning / Conscious / Linear / Local / Yang / Order / Predictable - Example: I desire to think of a red sports car and then I start imagining a red sports car. - Heart: - Intuitive Receiving / Unconscious / Non-Linear / Non-Local / Yin / Chaos / Unpredictable - Examples: - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and I receive no intuitive guidance. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a feelings that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a dream about a river flowing into the ocean which is symbolic, indirect and open to interpretation. - I am taking a shower, so I desire to get clean but I receive a random feeling that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry.

- The brain is a primary information-processing organ. - The heart sends more neural information to the brain than the brain sends to the heart. - The brain is not the sole commander b/c it is heavily influenced by—and often follows the rhythm of—the heart. - The brain is a powerful processor that normally follows the heart's rhythmic lead, not the other way around. - Brain vs Heart: - Brain: - Logical Reasoning / Conscious / Linear / Local / Yang / Order / Predictable - Example: I desire to think of a red sports car and then I start imagining a red sports car. - Heart: - Intuitive Receiving / Unconscious / Non-Linear / Non-Local / Yin / Chaos / Unpredictable - Examples: - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and I receive no intuitive guidance. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a feelings that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a dream about a river flowing into the ocean which is symbolic, indirect and open to interpretation. - I am taking a shower, so I desire to get clean but I receive a random feeling that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - In a coherent state (smooth heart rate variability, generate a sincere feeling of appreciation, care or compassion), the brain operates efficiently, creatively and with reduced internal conflict. - In an incoherent state, the brain's activity becomes erratic, fragmented and reactive. - The brain's cortical activity (especially in areas related to emotion and decision-making) synchronizes with the heart's rhythmic pattern.

- The heart's electromagnetic field acts as a central synchronizing signal that influences the brain (entrainment). - Intuition is the reception and processing of non-linear/non-local information via the heart's electromagnetic field. - The heart detects intuitive (non-local/non-linear) information before the brain does, then transmits it to the brain. - Heart coherence (smooth heart rate variability) enhances intuitive accuracy and cognitive function. - The energetic heart refers to the heart's dynamic, radiating electromagnetic field. - Intuition is heart based perception, radiating information from the energetic heart and to the body. - Intuition is often experienced as a "felt sense" or "gut feeling". - Intuitive signals are first detected by the heart (which responds to a future or distant event before the brain does) and then transmitted to the body/brain for processing.

- Non-linear is the perspective where the output is not directly proportional to the input. - Examples: - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry, on Monday I receive no intuitive guidance but on Tuesday I receive a feelings that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - During a shower on Wednesday I receive no intuitive guidance but on during a shower on Tuesday I receive a thought that explains exactly what I must do for a problem I've been struggling w/ at work for two weeks. - In a linear system, the same input always produces the same output. In a non-linear system, it does not. - Non-linear is less of an absolute truth and more of a description of perspective, b/c in life, creating the same input is difficult. Examples: - We may feel that the shower on Wednesday and Thursday were the exact same but they're not. Many things are different and one vital difference, is our heart coherence. - A dog may feel where it goes on walks is non-linear but the dog owner knows several factors influence this decision like the current temperature the current time of day and what is planned for the rest of the day. From the dog's perspective this decision is non-linear but from the dog owner's perspective this decision is linear. - Non-linear is a perspective in which outputs are not predictably proportional to inputs. - When we call a system "non-linear", we are saying: "I do not have enough information to predict its behavior" not "it is intrinsically unpredictable".

- Local means here (a specific point in space and time) and non-local means not here. - Example: The heart detects information about a future event (intuition). - Non-local is less of an absolute truth and more of a description of perspective. Examples: - The present and future may not seem connected but when we become aware of the causal relationship between them they become one. - From the perspective of the brain (conscious, linear, local), an intuitive feeling about someone across the room seems non-local but: - The heart generates an electromagnetic field that extends outside the body (measurable several feet away). - This field carries information about the heart's emotional and coherent state. - Other hearts (and brains) can detect this field, especially when both are coherent. - Thanks to the Earth's magnetic field (generated by the motion of molten iron in Earth's outer core), all is local, because the Earth's field surrounds us all. What seems non-local from a brain bound perspective (knowing something about a person in a different county) is local to the Earth's field (universal medium) and to our field which unites w/ the Earth's field. - Perspective determines what appears local or non-local. A wider field of view (heart, Earth) reveals connections that a narrower field (brain alone) cannot see. - Empathy (the felt sense of someone's emotional state) seems non-local from a brain perspective but from the heart's perspective, empathy is local. Local to the electromagnetic field that connects hearts. - When we call a phenomenon "non-local", we are saying: "I do not have enough information to trace the causal pathway" not "it is intrinsically non-local".

- Conscience is an inner sense of right and wrong. - Conscience is a moral awareness that guides behavior. - Consciousness is awareness and conscience is evaluative. - Conscience prompts us to avoid what we believe is wrong and do what we believe is right. - Conscience is often accompanied by feelings of guilt, approval or obligation. - Consciousness watches and conscience judges. - Not following our conscience produces feelings of guilt, shame or unease. - Following our conscience produces feelings of warmth, accomplishment and peace.

- Meditation is focus practice. - Meditation with heart coherence (smooth heart rate variability, generate a sincere feeling of appreciation, care or compassion) is amplified and more potent. - Meditation is a practice of focused attention.

- Mindfulness is the practice of paying attention, on purpose, to the present moment. - Mindfulness with coherence (smooth heart rate variability, generate a sincere feeling of appreciation, care or compassion) is the optimal state. - Mindfulness is present-moment awareness.

- Instinct is an innate (known from birth) behavior that is not learned. - Instinct is built into the nervous system of a species through evolution. - Instinct is triggered by specific stimuli and performed automatically without conscious deliberation. - Examples: - Humans have an innate capacity to learn grammar - The spider does not learn to spin a web; it just spins - A skill is learned through practice and an instinct is programmed at birth. - Instinct is an inherited and automatic behavior pattern.

- The nervous system is the body's electrical communication network, consisting of the brain, spinal cord and all peripheral nerves. - The nervous system is a bidirectional loop, with the heart sending massive amounts of information to the brain (via afferent neural pathways) that strongly influence perception, emotion and decision-making. - The heart's rhythmic pattern directly shapes the state of the entire nervous system. - Coherence in the heart produces a balanced, efficient nervous system. Incoherence produces a stressed and fragmented nervous system. - The nervous system is a two-way communication loop, heavily influenced upward by the heart's rhythmic signal.

- The sympathetic nervous system is often called the "fight or flight" branch. - The sympathetic nervous system activates the body for action. In response to perceived threat or stress it increases heart rate, raises blood pressure, slows digestion and releases stress hormones (adrenaline, cortisol). - While essential for survival, chronic sympathetic dominance leads to wear and tear on the body (allostatic load), erratic Heart Rate Variability and incoherence. - The sympathetic system speeds up the heart's rhythm without smooth variability. - Sympathetic is the stress-response branch that speeds up the heart and creates an incoherent rhythm when chronically active.

- The parasympathetic nervous system is often called the "rest and digest" branch. - The parasympathetic nervous system calms the body after stress: slowing heart rate, lowering blood pressure, promoting digestion and supporting recovery and repair. - The primary parasympathetic nerve is the vagus nerve, which connects directly from the brainstem to the heart. - High parasympathetic tone is associated with high HRV, resilience, emotional regulation and heart coherence. - The parasympathetic system slows the heart rhythm and enables the smooth, wave-like variability characteristic of coherence. - Parasympathetic is the rest-and-recovery branch that calms the heart and enables a coherent, resilient rhythm.

- Heart Coherence is a distinct, measurable physiological state characterized by a smooth, sine-wave-like pattern in heart rate variability (HRV)—the natural beat-to-beat changes in heart rhythm. - While in the coherence state: - The heart, brain and nervous system are synchronized. - There is a balance between the sympathetic (stress) and parasympathetic (relaxation) branches of the nervous system. - Cognitive function, emotional stability and immune response improves. - Individuals are better able to radiate genuine compassion and care. - Coherence is intentionally cultivated through techniques like the Heart Coherence Technique (focusing on a sincere positive emotion, such as appreciation or care, while breathing rhythmically). - Coherence is the ordered, harmonious and efficient functional state of the heart-rhythm-driven system. - Coherence in the heart produces a balanced, efficient nervous system. Incoherence produces a stressed and fragmented nervous system. - Heart Rate Variability is the natural beat-to-beat time change in your heart rhythm and is the key measurable signature of coherence.

- HRV (Heart Rate Variability) is the natural, healthy variation in time between successive heartbeats. - A healthy heart does not beat like a metronome (perfectly regular); it speeds up slightly on the inhale and slows down slightly on the exhale. - This variability is a key indicator of nervous system balance and overall health. - High ordered (sine-wave-like) HRV = coherence, resilience and emotional regulation. - Low or erratic HRV = stress, fatigue or illness. - HRV is the primary measurable metric used to assess heart coherence. - HRV is the natural beat-to-beat time change in your heart rhythm and is the key measurable signature of coherence.

- Afferent signals are messages from the heart (and body) to the brain. - The majority of vagus nerve traffic is afferent—meaning the heart sends more information to the brain than the brain sends to the heart. - The heart's afferent signals strongly influence brain activity, particularly in areas governing emotion, decision-making and perception. - The opposite of afferent is efferent: signals going from the central nervous system to the body.

- The vagus nerve is the primary neural highway connecting the brainstem to the heart, lungs and digestive tract. - The vagus nerve is the main conduit for parasympathetic (rest and digest) signals. - About 80-90% of vagus nerve fibers are afferent (carrying information from the heart and viscera to the brain). - Through the vagus nerve, the heart's rhythmic pattern and emotional state directly influence brain activity, particularly in regions associated with emotional regulation and social engagement. - A coherent heart rhythm is associated with healthy vagal tone (parasympathetic activity). - The vagus nerve is the primary physical cable connecting the heart's neural signals to the brain, mostly traveling heart to brain.

- Vagal tone is a measure of the activity level of the parasympathetic nervous system, specifically via the vagus nerve. - High vagal tone indicates a strong, resilient parasympathetic response: the ability to calm down quickly after stress, maintain emotional balance and exhibit high HRV (heart rate variability). - High vagal tone is associated with heart coherence. - Low vagal tone is associated with chronic stress, inflammation and erratic HRV. - Vagal tone can be improved through mindfulness, heart-focused breathing and feeling sincere positive emotions.

- Entrainment is the natural, physical tendency for two or more independent oscillators (rhythmic systems) to synchronize with the strongest or most dominant rhythm when they are coupled. - In the body, the heart is the dominant oscillator. Its rhythmic electromagnetic field entrains (pulls into sync) other bodily rhythms, including brain waves, respiratory rhythms and blood pressure oscillations. - While coherent (smooth heart rate variability, generate a sincere feeling of appreciation, care or compassion), this entrainment is smooth and harmonious. - Incoherence disrupts entrainment. - Between people, the stronger heart field can entrain a weaker one, especially when both are coherent and in proximity. - Entrainment is the heart's natural ability to synchronize other bodily rhythms to its own coherent beat.
The essence of something, is its fundamental qualities.

- God to some is: - Nonexistent. - The Supreme Being. - The Ultimate Reality. - The Primordial Vibration. - A living, pulsating and breathing field of energy that creates Souls as extensions of itself, sends them out to learn through experience and is enriched by their return. - How one defines God is a personal choice.

- Faith is trust, commitment or belief that is not based solely on logical proof or empirical evidence. - Faith is often involving confidence in a person, confidence in a doctrine and/or confidence in God. - Faith is what lets us board an airplane (trust in the pilot and engineers), marry someone (trust their character) and pray (trust that we are heard). - Faith is not the absence of doubt but action in spite of it.
Spirituality is the search for meaning, connection and transcendence.

- “Spiritual” refers to anything that relates to or arises from: - The search for meaning, connection, and transcendence. - Recognition of the Soul (Individual Consciousness Being / Spirit / Purusha) as a fragment of Source/God, sent out to learn through experience and return enriched. - Engagement with God. - Faith in God. - Practices that turn consciousness inward.
A Spirit is a Soul.

- A Soul (Individual Consciousness Being / Spirit / Purusha) is a fragment of the Source that thrusts outward, incarnates and returns. - A Soul is a child of the Source that is meant to return and become part of the Source. - God (Source / Aum) is the ultimate reality and Spirit is the individual fragment of that reality on its journey outward and inward. - You are a: - Soul (Individual Consciousness Being / Spirit / Purusha). - Fragment of God (Source) that has been sent out, is currently incarnated (on Earth, in a body) and is learning through experience. - You will eventually return to the Source and enrich all with your gained wisdom.

- Everyday Language Perspective: - The origin or starting point of something, examples: - Source of a problem: The root cause. - Water source: A spring, well, or river. - Light source: The Sun, a lamp, a candle. - Research Perspective: - Primary Source: Original, first-hand material from the time period being studied (e.g., diaries, letters, photographs, raw data, speeches). - Secondary Source: Information that analyzes or interprets primary sources (e.g., textbooks, biographies, review articles, documentaries). - Tertiary Source: A compilation or summary of primary and secondary sources (e.g., encyclopedias, almanacs, indexes). - Journalism Perspective: - The person, document, or organization that provides information for a news story. - On-the-record source: Can be named and quoted directly. - Anonymous/Confidential source: Identity is hidden (e.g., "a White House insider"). - Computer Programming Perspective: - Source code: The human-readable instructions written by a programmer (e.g., Rust, TypeScript, HTML code). - Data source: The origin of data (e.g., a database, API, file, or sensor). - Spiritual Perspective: - Source is God. - A source is the origin/provider of something.

- Aum (also written as Oṁ or āuṁ) is the primordial vibration of the universe and the vibrational name of the Supreme Being. - The proper way to repeat Aum is with breath focus (prāṇāyāma) and deep devotional feeling. - When Aum is repeated properly it: - Turns our consciousness inward. - Conserves psychological energy. - Triggers energetic transformation. - Lifts the yogi beyond worldly obstacles and into the realm of spirit. - Properly repeating Aum does not remove obstacles; it lifts us to a level where they no longer apply.

- The Tao (pronounced "Dow") that can be told is not the eternal Tao. This means: The Tao cannot be fully captured in words or concepts but the following is our best attempt. - The Tao is the natural, spontaneous, eternal way that underlies, unites and flows through all things. - The Tao is the "way" of nature, the rhythm of existence and the path that cannot be forced. - We cannot grasp the Tao but we can live it. - The Tao is the unnamable, spontaneous, creative way of the universe—to be lived, not just understood.

- Wu wei (pronounced "woo way") means effortless action, non forcing and acting in such a way that one's actions are in complete harmony with the natural flow of things. - Wu wei is NOT: - Laziness. - Passivity. - Doing nothing. - Giving up. - Wu wei IS: - Action so skillful, so spontaneous, so perfectly timed that it feels effortless. - Acting without forcing, without straining, without self-conscious effort. - The state of "flow" described by athletes, artists and musicians when they are "in the zone". - Wu wei is how the Tao acts. - The Tao does not try to make spring follow winter; it simply does. The river does not try to flow downhill; it simply does. - Wu wei is aligning oneself with that same spontaneous, effortless creativity. - Wu wei is action: - Without forcing. - That is effortless, spontaneous, perfectly aligned with the way things are.

- Yin and Yang are the two complementary, interdependent and dynamic polarities that constitute all aspects of reality. - Yin and Yang are not opposites in conflict but partners in dance. - Yin: 1. Nature: Receptive, passive, yielding. 2. Direction: Downward, inward, contracting. 3. Energy: Dark, cool, moist, heavy. 4. Substance: Earth, water, moon. 5. Breath: Exhale 6. Season: Winter. - Yang: 1. Nature: Active, assertive, moving. 2. Direction: Upward, outward, expanding. 3. Energy: Light, warm, dry, light. 4. Substance: Sky, fire, sun. 5. Breath: Inhale 6. Season: Summer. - Principles: - Interdependence: Yin and yang define each other. There is no "up" without "down," no "dark" without "light." - Mutual containment: Yin contains the seed of yang; yang contains the seed of yin (the dots in the tai chi symbol). - Dynamic balance: When one reaches its extreme, it transforms into the other. Day becomes night; winter becomes summer. - Harmony, not conflict: The goal is not the victory of one over the other but their harmonious interplay. - Yin vs Yang: - Yang: - Logical Reasoning / Conscious / Brain / Linear / Local / Order / Predictable - Example: I desire to think of a red sports car and then I start imagining a red sports car. - Yin: - Intuitive Receiving / Unconscious / Heart / Non-Linear / Non-Local / Chaos / Unpredictable - Examples: - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and I receive no intuitive guidance. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a feelings that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry. - I desire to know if Rose is the one I should marry and then I receive a dream about a river flowing into the ocean which is symbolic, indirect and open to interpretation. - I am taking a shower, so I desire to get clean but I receive a random feeling that I interpret as a clear knowing that Rose is the one that I should marry - The art of living is not to eliminate one but to balance both.
- Eternal means without beginning or end. - Eternal exists outside of time. - Something eternal lasts for all time without cessation. - Immortal has no death like eternal but does have a birth.
- Grace is goodness that surprises. - Grace is the beauty of a kind word when we expected a deserved rebuke. - Examples: Second chance, friend who forgives us before we finish apologizing.

- Mercy is compassion or forbearance shown toward someone whom it is within one's power to punish. - Justice is giving what is deserved and mercy is withholding the punishment that is deserved. - Mercy is what we naturally hope for when we have done wrong and cannot undo it. - Mercy is not choosing punishment but instead choosing compassion.

- Beauty is the quality that gives pleasure, meaning or a sense of harmony to the perceiver. - Kinds of beauty: - Natural (e.g., mountain, flower, tiger, face) - Artistic (e.g., painting, symphony, poem, dance) - Mathematical (e.g., the elegance of a proof, the symmetry of a fractal) - Moral (e.g., courage, forgiveness, sacrifice) - Beauty is often associated with symmetry, proportion, vividness and emotional resonance.

- Dance is the art of moving the body in a rhythmic, patterned and often expressive way—usually in relation to music, space and sometimes other dancers. - A good relationship is like a dance: sometimes leading, sometimes following, always responding. - Wu wei is dancing: you do not think about each step; you simply move. - Yin and yang are the dance of opposites: forward/back, lift/release, tension/ease. - Dance is what happens when our body listens to our heart and the music at the same time.
- Music is the art of organizing sound in time—using pitch, rhythm, dynamics and timbre to create beauty, express emotion and/or convey meaning. - Music is organized sound that moves our body.
